event: wife and two daughters had been diagnosed with influenza B virus infection
rapid test using a nasal swab specimen was positive for influenza B antigens
diagnosed with an influenza B virus infection
oxygen saturation level was 88% on room air
Fine crackles were audible in lung fields on both sides
PaO2 = 72 Torr under the 5 L/min of nasal oxygen inhalation
chest radiography showed ground-glass opacity in lung fields on both sides
chest computed tomography showed diffuse ground-glass opacity in lung fields on both sides
Gram staining of sputum samples did not show any evidence of bacterial or fungal infection
Ziehl-Neelsen staining of sputum samples did not show any evidence of bacterial or fungal infection
periodic acid-Schiff stain of sputum samples did not show any evidence of bacterial or fungal infection
sputum culture did not show any evidence of bacterial or fungal infection
no evidence of bacterial infection in blood cultures
reticular shadow on chest CT worsened
diagnosed with severe ARDS associated with influenza B virus infection
mechanical ventilation and high levels of positive end-expiratory pressure proposed
Polymyxin B-immobilized fiber column hemoperfusion was performed
reticular infiltrates on chest CT worsened
diffuse alveolar damage with prominent hyaline membranes on lung pathology
